reasoning: The historical base rate of new WHO pandemic declarations is extremely low (only two in the past 20 years: H1N1 in 2009 and COVID-19 in 2020). The Polymarket odds were around 9% in late May 2025, likely driven in part by ongoing concerns regarding the H5N1 avian influenza outbreak, which has caused human infections (including a US fatality in January 2025). However, multiple expert sources and public health updates through May 2025 confirm there is currently no evidence of sustained human-to-human transmission of H5N1. Without human-to-human transmission, the timeline for the virus to mutate, spread globally across multiple WHO regions, and prompt an official pandemic declaration before the end of December 2025 is very truncated. The timeline from initial human-to-human outbreak to a formal pandemic declaration is typically a few months, and since we are already in June 2025 with zero sustained transmission chains, the window for a 2025 declaration is closing rapidly. Much of the recent news volume for 'WHO pandemic 2025' relates to the signing of the pandemic preparedness treaty at the World Health Assembly in May, not an active pandemic. Given the shortened time horizon and strict criteria required for a formal WHO declaration, my estimate is slightly below the May market price.
